name: Capital World
uniqueTerrain: Faction-specific terrain layout — Terran: urban/industrial districts, Shards: crystalline formations woven into infrastructure, Horde: junkyard wastelands of repurposed wreckage, Revenant: tomb-architecture corridors of ancient obsidian, Accord: diplomatic compounds with interconnected biodomes
terrain: Dense urban sprawl dominates every Capital World — multi-story buildings provide garrison positions with interlocking fields of fire, wide avenues funnel vehicles into kill zones while offering assault corridors, elevated overpasses and mag-rail bridges create critical height advantage positions, and underground tunnel networks allow infantry flanking routes beneath the surface. Destructible environments are key: buildings collapse under sustained fire, creating rubble cover that reshapes the battlefield mid-engagement. Expect fortified government districts, industrial sectors with heavy machinery cover, civilian zones with narrow alleys, and orbital defense battery emplacements that can be captured and turned against attackers.
visual: From orbit, a Capital World blazes with light — city grids cover 40%+ of the surface in geometric patterns of civilization. Orbital rings of defense platforms, shipyards, and commercial stations form a visible halo. The atmosphere carries a permanent industrial haze, punctuated by the glow of massive energy reactors. Approaching fleets see a world wrapped in the infrastructure of empire — comm-sat constellations, debris fields from centuries of orbital construction, and the ever-present patrol routes of defense flotillas.
special: Always faction-controlled at campaign start — never spawns neutral or contested. Territory count is fixed per faction but scales with difficulty setting and faction choice (PH values per faction). Capital tag is permanent — recapture of a lost capital is always a strategic priority. Defender’s advantage mechanic: auto-militia garrison, orbital defense batteries, and morale bonus for defending forces. Losing a Capital World triggers a faction-wide morale crisis. Orbital bombardment is possible but destroys infrastructure permanently, reducing post-capture yield by 50% (PH). Siege warfare can last 20+ cycles.
narrative: The beating heart of a civilization. Assaulting a Capital World is not a battle — it is a CAMPAIGN. Sixteen to twenty-two territories of increasingly fortified urban terrain where every city block is a fortress, every intersection is a kill zone, and every underground passage hides defenders who know every centimeter of their home. Capitals don’t fall to brute force. They fall to attrition, cunning, and the willingness to pay the price in blood. The faction that holds the most Capital Worlds holds the galaxy.

name: Terrestrial

subtypes.name: Temperate
subtypes.terrain: Rolling grasslands, scattered deciduous forests, gentle river valleys. The most balanced terrain — open fields for ranged combat, forest patches for cover, river crossings as natural chokepoints. Weather cycles between clear skies, rain, and fog. Moderate visibility in all conditions.
subtypes.visual: Earth-like beauty — deep blue oceans, green continents, white clouds. The archetypal habitable world. City lights on the night side, agricultural patterns on temperate zones.

subtypes.name: Arid-Temperate
subtypes.terrain: Dry savanna and scrubland with sparse tree cover. More open than temperate — longer sightlines, less natural cover. Dried riverbeds provide movement corridors. Rocky outcrops serve as strongpoints. Dust devils reduce visibility locally. Vehicles have better mobility than in dense terrain.
subtypes.visual: Golden-brown continents with minimal green. Vast open plains under a hazy sky. Thin cloud cover, bright sunlight. Resembles the African savanna from orbit — dry but not dead.

subtypes.name: Tropical
subtypes.terrain: Dense tropical forests, wide river deltas, coastal wetlands. Higher vegetation density than temperate — closer to Jungle but with more clearings and settled areas. Frequent rain reduces visibility. River networks segment the battlefield. High humidity degrades unsealed equipment over time.
subtypes.visual: Lush green landmasses with extensive river networks visible from orbit. Thick cloud bands over equatorial regions. Deep blue-green oceans. The most biodiverse terrestrial variant — rain forests cover 60%+ of land area.

terrain: The most tactically balanced terrain in the galaxy — open grasslands provide clean sightlines for ranged engagements, scattered forests offer light cover and concealment for ambush tactics, river crossings create natural chokepoints that reward defensive positioning, and rolling hills provide moderate height advantage without the extreme verticality of mountain worlds. Mixed terrain forces diverse army compositions: pure infantry struggles in open fields, pure vehicles bog down in forests, and balanced forces dominate. Weather patterns shift between clear skies (favoring ranged), rain (reducing visibility), and fog banks (infantry ambush conditions).
visual: Earth-like beauty from orbit — deep blue oceans covering 60–70% of the surface, green and brown continental landmasses with visible mountain ranges, white cloud formations tracking weather systems. These are the worlds every faction recognizes as ‘home’ regardless of origin. Scattered city lights on the night side reveal moderate population density. Visible agricultural patterns on temperate continents.
special: 40% random encounter chance per cycle — the galaxy’s most contested real estate draws attention from everyone. Balanced resource yield makes them universally valuable: no faction can afford to ignore them, no faction gains disproportionate advantage from holding them. Soft advantages for factions whose biology or technology suits temperate environments, but no hard mechanical bonuses — advantages emerge from terrain interaction, not stat modifiers. Additional strategic upsides pending balance pass (PH). Distribution weighted by star type for realistic galaxy feel. The ‘standard’ planet against which all other combat modifiers are measured — baseline encounters, baseline terrain, baseline everything.
narrative: Control the majority of Terrestrial worlds and you control the galactic economy. Every faction wants them. Every faction fights to hold them. They are the backbone of any empire — not the most dramatic battlefields, but the ones that decide who wins the war. Terrestrial campaigns are wars of attrition: slow, grinding, and ultimately determined by who has better logistics and more reserves.

name: Volcanic
terrain: A hellscape of obsidian ridges providing hard cover that shatters under heavy weapons fire, static lava flows creating impassable channels across the battlefield, volcanic vents that pulse with AOE damage during eruption events (PH values), and ash clouds that reduce accuracy during active eruptions. The ground itself is unstable — seismic tremors can collapse tunnel systems and shift terrain elevation mid-battle. Random eruption events redirect molten rivers and reshape the battlefield dynamically. Environmental damage comes from events (eruptions, vent bursts, lava flow shifts), not passive atmospheric effects.
visual: An angry world of orange-red surface glow visible from deep space — active lava flows trace luminous arterial patterns across the dark volcanic surface. Mountain chains of active volcanoes belch columns of ash into a thick, choking atmosphere. The sky is a permanent twilight of sulfurous haze punctuated by the red glow of eruptions. From orbit, the planet pulses like a burning coal, its surface a constantly shifting mosaic of cooling obsidian and fresh magma.
special: 20% chance of Rich Veins trait (doubles Scrap yield — PH values). Highest pure Scrap income of any planet type, plus geothermal energy bonus (E yield — PH value), but zero Intel and zero Influence — a resource world, not a cultural or intelligence hub. Factions with sealed or mechanical units naturally fare better in volcanic conditions. Normal building capacity plus specialized slots for geothermal infrastructure (Geothermal Plant). Static lava fields are impassable terrain; random eruption events create dynamic hazards that reshape the battlefield.
narrative: Resource-rich but brutally hostile. Volcanic worlds are the forge-worlds of the galaxy — mountains of raw material locked behind environments that kill the unprepared. Perfect for factions that thrive in harsh conditions, and a death sentence for those that don’t. The eruptions don’t discriminate — every faction pays the same price in fire. Only those who adapt to the rhythm of the planet survive long enough to extract its riches.

name: Ocean/Archipelago
terrain: Island warfare defines every engagement — landmasses of varying size connected by bridges, causeways, and shallow fords create a patchwork of defensible positions separated by water. Some islands are large enough to support full vehicle deployment and combined-arms operations. Movement between islands uses drop pods, transport ships, or bridges and causeways depending on what the map and player have available. Beach chokepoints funnel attackers into pre-sighted kill zones. Dense tropical interiors provide concealment once past the beaches. Periodic tidal flooding events submerge low-lying territories, forcing units to higher ground.
visual: A water world — 90%+ ocean surface with scattered island chains and atolls visible from orbit like scattered emeralds on deep blue silk. Planet-spanning tropical storm systems track across the ocean, their spiral arms covering entire hemispheres. Shallow reefs create turquoise halos around island groups. The few settlements cluster on the largest islands, their structures built to withstand the constant storms. From orbit, the planet appears almost entirely blue, broken only by the white of cloud systems and the dark green of island vegetation.
special: 15% chance of Flux Deposit trait (+3 Intel per cycle — deep ocean currents carry exotic particles useful for intelligence gathering). Air superiority is critical: limited land-based anti-air installations can cover the water gaps between islands, making air units a dominant force projection tool. Transport capability becomes the most strategically valuable asset — controlling who can move between islands determines who controls the planet. Factions with strong transport or air capability have natural advantages in projecting power across water gaps.
narrative: Ocean worlds force a fundamentally different style of warfare. Island-hopping campaigns, amphibious assaults, and air dominance battles replace the ground-based attrition of continental warfare. Factions without transport capacity struggle to project power across the water gaps — they risk being trapped on whatever island they land on while enemies island-hop around them. The ocean is not empty space; it is an obstacle that only the prepared can cross efficiently.

name: Jungle
terrain: Multi-layered canopy warfare — visibility restriction is per-territory and per-unit, varying with canopy density. Some territories feature dense canopy with severely limited sightlines, while clearings and river banks offer normal visibility. Vine-covered ruins from pre-spaceflight civilizations provide fortifiable positions with ancient stone walls. River crossings create natural chokepoints where the canopy thins enough for limited air support. All non-infantry units suffer movement penalties as roots, vines, and soft ground impede mechanical locomotion. Dense canopy blocks orbital reconnaissance, making fog-of-war persistent until ground units physically enter a territory.
visual: From orbit, an unbroken emerald canopy stretches from pole to pole — the jungle is so dense that no ground is visible from space. Massive river systems thread silver paths through the endless green, the only landmarks in an ocean of vegetation. Frequent tropical storms dump torrential rain that feeds the explosive growth. At night, bioluminescent organisms create ghostly blue-green light patterns visible from low orbit. The atmosphere is thick with moisture, creating a permanent haze layer that softens the world’s edges.
special: Highest encounter rate of any standard planet type at 50% — the galaxy’s most dangerous exploration environment. 25% chance of Indigenous Species event (primitive but hostile natives). 20% chance hostile fauna appears in tactical battles as a neutral third force. Infantry-heavy armies excel in close quarters; vehicle-heavy armies struggle with mobility penalties. Guaranteed dense vegetation modifiers shape every engagement — the jungle itself dictates the terms of battle. Zero Influence yield — the jungle doesn’t care about politics.
narrative: Every territory assault is an expedition into hostile terrain. The environment is as dangerous as the enemy — and the enemy knows how to use it. Jungle worlds consume armies that aren’t prepared for them. Vehicles rust in the humidity, electronics short in the rain, and the canopy swallows orbital surveillance like a green black hole. The factions that master jungle warfare gain access to Intel-rich worlds that others cannot hold. Those that don’t learn to fight in the green hell simply disappear into it.

name: Desert
terrain: Varied terrain zones each with distinct tactical properties — open sand plains provide minimal cover but excellent vehicle mobility, rocky mesas rise abruptly to offer commanding height advantage, deep canyons create natural chokepoints where a few defenders can hold against many, and salt flats offer extreme long-range sightlines. Range bonuses are terrain-specific: dunes offer moderate advantage, canyons compress engagements, salt flats maximize ranged firepower. Heat effects are terrain-specific and weather-event-driven (heatwaves), not passive atmospheric damage. Vehicle-friendly flat terrain means armored forces dominate the open ground between terrain features.
visual: A world of harsh beauty — endless sand seas ripple to the horizon under a white-hot sun, interrupted by massive rocky mesas that cast long shadows across the dunes. Dry canyon systems cut deep into the sandstone, their walls layered in millions of years of geological history. No surface water exists. The atmosphere is crystal-clear between storms, offering unlimited visibility, then drops to near-zero when the sand walls hit. At sunset, the entire world turns gold and red.
special: 15% chance of Buried Cache trait (one-time salvage of scrap from pre-collapse supply depots buried under the sand — PH values). Niche resource value — rich in one specific resource but limited in others. Ranged combat dominance on open terrain, but canyon and mesa fighting rewards different approaches. Sandstorms affect range, shields, and other combat factors. Scanning mechanic can uncover buried structures, ruins, or technology beneath the sand. The desert strips warfare to its essentials: move fast, shoot far, hold the high ground.
narrative: Desert warfare is about MOBILITY and POSITION. Units in the open are dead. Controlling mesas and canyon mouths determines who wins — and who dies in the sand. The desert strips warfare to its essentials: move fast, shoot far, hold the high ground. Factions that bring the right tools dominate. Factions that don’t find their armies bleaching in the sun.

name: Ice World
terrain: Movement effects are terrain-specific — frozen lakes create hazards for heavy vehicles risking fall-through, glaciers provide elevated hard cover with treacherous approaches, and tundra plains offer open sightlines with scattered boulder cover. Snow drifts reduce infantry speed in affected territories. Ice caves provide underground flanking routes but risk collapse under artillery bombardment. High base visibility across the frozen landscape; blizzards temporarily flip conditions to extremely low visibility, turning ranged engagements into point-blank encounters. Cold debuffs only activate during environmental events (blizzards, ice storms), not as passive effects.
visual: Continental ice sheets stretch from pole to pole, broken only by glacial mountain ranges that thrust jagged peaks through the frozen surface. Frozen oceans show stress fractures visible from orbit as dark lines across white expanses. The thin, clear atmosphere between storms reveals brilliant polar auroras dancing across the sky. During blizzards, the entire world disappears under swirling white. At the equator, brief summer thaws create temporary liquid water that refreezes into treacherous thin-ice zones.
special: 20% chance of Cryo-Vault trait (one-time discovery of preserved ancient technology locked in ice — can provide tech tree shortcuts or unique equipment). Can contain Revenant sleeper caches in addition to standard Ancient Ruins discoveries. High base visibility across open ice fields; blizzard events temporarily flip conditions to near-zero visibility. The hostile environment challenges all factions during weather events — those with sealed, insulated, or cold-adapted units have natural advantages, but no faction is immune to the ice.
narrative: Ice Worlds test logistics as much as tactics. The cold is a second enemy that strikes during storms and blizzards — it slows movement, obscures vision, and makes every battle more expensive than the last. Campaigns here are wars of endurance: whoever can sustain their forces through the worst conditions wins, regardless of tactical skill. Between storms, the frozen landscape offers stark beauty and long sightlines. When the blizzards hit, survival becomes the only objective.

name: Ancient Ruins
terrain: Massive megastructures from a civilization that predates all current factions by millennia — 10m-thick walls that shrug off conventional weapons, vehicle-width corridors that channel movement into predictable paths, underground chamber networks that extend for kilometers. Partially functional systems add chaos: active energy fields that damage anything passing through, collapsed sections that create impassable rubble zones, and automated defense encounters (turrets, drones, barriers) based on world state — Great Filter security remnants that activate unpredictably and attack all sides. The architecture defies conventional military doctrine — rooms are sized for beings far larger than any current species.
visual: From orbit, the remains of an impossibly advanced civilization scar the surface — continental-scale geometric patterns etched into the landscape by engineering beyond current comprehension. Some structures still glow with residual power after thousands of years. The atmosphere is artificially maintained by systems that no living faction understands. At ground level, the scale is overwhelming: doorways 20 meters tall, corridors wide enough for armored columns, and machinery whose purpose can only be guessed at. Holographic glyphs still flicker in the darkness.
special: ALWAYS contain lore fragments — the primary resource for the Unification victory path. Guaranteed Revenant sleepers present, but their state depends on a combination of factors (random roll, exposure time, planet traits). Sleepers require POWER + TIME to wake — not instant activation. Every Ancient Ruins world guarantees at least one tech discovery; Precursor Cache trait gives bonus or rarer tech. Higher chance of Guardian patrols — not all ruins are Revenant, some are pre-Revenant Aethyn sites. Highest encounter rate at 70% — these worlds attract every faction, pirate group, and independent scavenger in the sector. Revenant player gets unique bonus: faster excavation, access to systems other factions can’t use, and the ability to wake sleepers that would be permanently lost to others — this is how the Revenant bolsters their army since they cannot build units traditionally. Fighting inside the ruins negates most conventional advantages — vehicle superiority means nothing in corridors.
narrative: The galaxy’s libraries, museums, and mysteries — all locked behind the most dangerous exploration environments in known space. Ancient Ruins are the primary source of lore fragments required for the Unifier ending path, making them strategically essential for any faction pursuing that victory condition. But the 70% encounter rate means you’re never alone in the ruins. Every faction, every scavenger, every opportunist in the sector is drawn to these worlds. The ruins don’t just contain knowledge — they attract conflict like a magnet attracts iron.

name: Gas Giant
terrain: Orbital and atmospheric platform warfare — territories are individual platforms that players board and capture. All combat takes place on artificial structures suspended in the upper atmosphere of a world with no solid surface. Mixed platform types: some are large enough for vehicle deployment, others are infantry-only depending on platform size and construction. Enclosed walkways limit movement to narrow corridors, open bridges expose units to high winds that push lightweight units toward lethal edges. Units knocked off platforms are destroyed instantly — they fall into a crushing atmosphere with no recovery. Wind shear on exposed platforms pushes units toward edges during storm events. All cover is constructed, not natural: barricades, bulkheads, storage containers.
visual: A massive banded atmosphere dominates the view — swirling bands of amber, cream, and rust stretch across a world hundreds of times larger than any terrestrial planet. No solid surface exists. Multiple moons orbit in visible proximity. Planet-spanning storm systems larger than entire terrestrial worlds churn in the atmosphere. Mining platforms and orbital stations appear as tiny specks against the enormous backdrop, connected by thread-thin transit lines. The view from a platform edge is vertigo made real: an infinite fall into crushing pressure and darkness.
special: Cannot be colonized traditionally — only orbital platforms and atmospheric mining rigs provide habitable space. Platforms can be DESTROYED instead of captured for strategic denial — the resource is lost permanently. Atmospheric hazards affect space combat in orbit (gravity wells, radiation). High energy yield, low everything else — pure industrial extraction. Combat is cramped, dangerous, and claustrophobic on smaller platforms; larger platforms support combined-arms operations. Lighter, faster units are paradoxically more vulnerable due to wind-push effects near platform edges.
narrative: Fighting on atmospheric platforms is a nightmare of tight corridors, lethal drops, and the constant awareness that the floor beneath you is a pressurized lid over an infinite abyss. One wrong step, one well-placed explosion near an edge, and units simply cease to exist — swallowed by an atmosphere that would crush a starship. No one fights for Gas Giants because they want to. They fight because the resources are too valuable to ignore and too dangerous to extract peacefully.

name: Moon
terrain: Low gravity fundamentally changes combat physics — increased movement speed for all units as reduced gravity allows longer strides and faster acceleration. Projectile arcs flatten significantly, extending effective range for ballistic weapons (PH values). Craters of all sizes provide natural cover bowls — units inside craters are protected from direct fire but vulnerable to plunging attacks. No atmosphere means no sound: visual cues replace audio warnings, making ambushes more effective. Dust kicked up by movement hangs in the airless environment for extended periods, creating visual obscuration.
visual: A small airless body scarred by eons of impact craters — from sharp-rimmed fresh impacts to ancient erosion-smoothed basins. Low-gravity dust plains catch and reflect the light of the parent planet, creating an ethereal silver-gray landscape. Without atmosphere, shadows are absolute black against blinding white sunlit surfaces. The parent planet dominates the sky, a massive presence that serves as a constant directional reference. Bootprints and vehicle tracks persist indefinitely in the undisturbed regolith.
special: Low Gravity environment is guaranteed on every Moon — the physics changes are always in effect, not a random trait. Moons can have subtypes (jungle moon, ice moon, etc.) with moon type influenced by parent planet and system type. Strategic value tied to parent planet: staging area for invasion, orbital advantage, forward operating base for siege operations. Meteor showers and other events can create additional environmental conditions. Bigger moons around gas giants can function like small planets with better resources. Quick to conquer (3–5 territories) but quick to lose — low territory count means a single successful assault can flip control.
narrative: Quick skirmishes on small battlefields — 3 to 5 territories that can be conquered in a single session. The resource yield is minimal, but the strategic value is everything. A Moon in the right orbit is worth more than a Terrestrial world in the wrong system. Lunar warfare is fast, violent, and decisive: there’s nowhere to retreat, nowhere to hide for long, and the low gravity turns every engagement into a high-speed ballet of death.

name: Orbital Station
terrain: Interior corridor warfare at its most intense — narrow passageways barely wide enough for two units abreast, sealed bulkheads that can be locked to create chokepoints, open cargo bays that serve as the only ‘wide’ combat spaces, reactor rooms where stray fire risks catastrophic decompression, and maintenance shafts that allow single-file flanking routes. NO vehicles can be deployed. NO air units can operate. This is infantry-only warfare in tight, claustrophobic environments where melee contact is nearly guaranteed within the first 30 seconds of any engagement. Breaching charges on bulkheads and airlocks are the primary tactical tool.
visual: An artificial structure hanging in the void — not a world but a construction of metal, composite, and purpose. From outside, a sprawling complex of modules, docking arms, communication arrays, and solar panels. Interior spaces range from cramped maintenance corridors lit by emergency lighting to expansive command centers with viewports showing the stars. The station creaks and groans with the stress of orbital mechanics. Every surface is functional: conduits, panels, access hatches. Nothing is decorative. The vacuum of space is always one hull breach away.
special: Stations are DESTRUCTIBLE from space — skip ground combat entirely but lose all contents and resources permanently. Found at warp lane junctions AND scattered randomly throughout the galaxy. Players can potentially BUILD new stations (PH decision — costs, requirements, and limitations all placeholder). Specialized station types: trade (economy bonus), military (fleet repair), research (tech acceleration), sensor (intel range), salvage (Scrap-Horde bonus). May contain critical infrastructure: ship repair facilities, communication arrays, or orbital defense platforms. Decompression events can occur from structural damage, instantly killing all unsealed units in affected sections. Variable yield reflects the station’s function and condition.
narrative: The most intense infantry-focused engagements in the game. Every corner is a potential ambush. Every sealed door may have enemies behind it. Every wall is a potential breach point. Station warfare strips away everything except close-quarters skill — no vehicles, no air support, no terrain advantage, no long-range doctrine. Just soldiers, weapons, and corridors. The choice to destroy a station from orbit is always tempting — but what you lose in resources and infrastructure may cost more than the lives you save.

name: Dead World
terrain: High visibility across open terrain with long sightlines in all directions, BUT craters and canyons provide meaningful cover positions. Scattered boulders offer hard cover, impact craters provide defilade, canyon systems create chokepoints and flanking routes, and ancient wreckage from whatever catastrophe killed this world creates improvised fortifications. Not completely flat — the scarred landscape offers more tactical variety than it first appears. Vehicles dominate open ground, but infantry can exploit the broken terrain for ambush positions.
visual: A world that died — barren, lifeless surface stretching to every horizon without a single living thing. No atmosphere to scatter light means the sky is black even during the day, stars visible alongside the local sun. No water, no vegetation, no movement except the slow drift of ancient dust. The surface is scarred by whatever killed it: impact craters, vitrified glass plains from orbital bombardment, or the slow erosion of a world that simply ran out of time. From orbit, it’s a gray-brown sphere of nothing — the galaxy’s tombstone.
special: No distinction between naturally dead and stripped worlds — dead is dead, regardless of cause. Re-Origination candidate to a specific type (PH system — costs, timelines, and target types all placeholder). Costs resources for Re-Origination; worthless to hold without Re-Origination except for warp lane control. Cheapest to claim and fortify. 40% chance of Derelict Fleet trait (salvageable or reactivatable ships — can provide significant military assets if recovered). 15% chance of hazardous environmental effects (radiation zones, unstable ground, toxic residue). 60% encounter rate — surprisingly high for a worthless-looking world, because Dead Worlds near the galactic core may contain Guardian ruins and pre-collapse technology.
narrative: The galaxy’s graveyards. Something happened here — war, ecological collapse, stellar event, or something worse. The remains tell stories to those willing to dig, and the 60% encounter rate proves that plenty of factions are willing to dig. Dead Worlds are the ultimate risk-reward proposition: terrible resource yield, dangerous environmental hazards, but the chance of finding a Derelict Fleet or Guardian ruins makes them worth the gamble. Every Dead World is a mystery. Not all mysteries have pleasant answers.

name: Ring World
terrain: Artificial landscape on the inner surface of a massive ring structure with a fabricated micro-star burning at its core and a half-Dyson collector shell wrapped around one hemisphere of the star. The collector serves dual purpose — harvesting stellar energy through conduit networks that feed power to the entire ring, and casting a moving shadow across the habitat as the ring rotates, creating a natural day/night cycle for the biome strips below. Each territory is a radically different environment separated by shimmering atmospheric energy barriers that regulate climate between zones — invisible from orbit, but cross one on foot and the air changes temperature, humidity, and pressure in a single step. Forest strips with trees that have grown unchecked for millennia, their canopies forming dark clusters visible from orbit. Ocean strips where tidal patterns follow centrifugal physics, not lunar gravity, with coastal shallows where sand shows through turquoise water. Desert strips with dune ridges casting long shadows, where barrier failures let the artificial star’s unfiltered radiation scorch the surface. The scale breaks the brain — horizons curve UP, the sky is the other side of the ring, and at the center burns a star that someone BUILT with an engineering shell cradling half of it. Combat across biome boundaries means adapting to radical environmental shifts mid-engagement, and the energy barriers themselves become strategic assets — disable one and two biomes bleed into each other, reshaping the battlefield. Fighting on the night side means fighting in the collector’s shadow — reduced visibility, cooler temperatures, different tactical conditions than the sunlit half.
visual: From deep space, the Ring World announces itself — a bright halo of reflected light surrounding a star that shouldn’t exist, half-cradled in a dark metallic shell covered in geometric panel lines and pulsing blue energy conduits. Sensors confirm what the eye refuses to accept: the star is artificial, a sustained fusion reaction held in place by Aethyn engineering that has been burning for a hundred thousand years. The half-Dyson collector wrapping its far side glows faintly orange on its inner surface where it drinks the star’s energy, while its outer face is cold industrial metal — the starkest possible contrast to the living paradise it powers. The inner surface of the ring is a patchwork of real terrain visible from orbit — deep blue oceans, dense green forest canopies, sandy desert dunes, and the faint shimmer of energy barriers marking biome transitions. City lights cluster along coastlines and river systems. Some sections are dark — collapsed ecosystems or zones the Guardians have sealed. Docking stations with colored navigation lights dot the outer structural framework, connected to the habitat band by cross-bracing struts. The Aethyn didn’t just build a habitat. They built a solar system — complete with an engineered day/night cycle. One structure at a time.
special: Highest resource yield of any world type — a single Ring World can sustain an entire war effort. The artificial star at its core provides limitless energy, harvested by the half-Dyson collector shell and distributed through conduit networks embedded in the ring’s structural framework. Small territory count (4–8) means quick to capture, but the VALUE is immense and every faction in the galaxy will come for it. Ancient automated systems may still be partially functional — maintenance drones, atmospheric processors, stellar regulators keeping the artificial sun stable, and the collector’s energy distribution grid. Biome Collapse failure events can permanently destroy territories. Stellar Instability events risk the artificial star itself — if its containment fails, the Ring World dies. Collector Disruption events can lock the ring in permanent daylight or darkness, drastically altering biome conditions. Cannot contain Revenant sleepers (pre-dates them — pure Aethyn, pre-Fracture). Guardian interest is HIGH. Aethyn origin — Eternal Shards receive unique faction discoveries. For the Unifier path, restoring Ring World systems contributes to rebuilding the Aethyn infrastructure network.
narrative: The Dyson Sphere harvests a star. The Ring World BUILT one — and then wrapped half of it in an engineering shell that simultaneously powers the ring and gives its inhabitants the mercy of nightfall. This is what the Aethyn were — a civilization that looked at the raw fabric of physics and said ‘we can do better.’ An artificial sun with its own collector lampshade, burning steady for a hundred thousand years, surrounded by a ring of engineered biomes where forests have canopies older than most civilizations and oceans follow physics that don’t exist on any natural world. Finding a Ring World doesn’t just change the balance of power. It changes the question. Every faction that sees one asks the same thing: ‘What kind of civilization builds its own sun and then shades half of it so its children can sleep?’ And then: ‘What destroyed them?’ A Ring World is not just a strategic asset. It is proof that perfection existed — and that even perfection wasn’t enough.

name: Dyson Sphere
terrain: N/A — the Dyson Sphere is not a ground combat environment. It is a system-level megastructure: a geometric cage of interlocking panels, structural lattice, and energy conduit networks wrapped around an imprisoned star. The entire structure spans roughly the diameter of a planetary orbit — panels the size of continents bolted to structural beams that dwarf most moons. Three equatorial distribution rings channel harvested energy outward through relay beams to stations positioned throughout the system, each ring rotating on a different axis to maintain continuous coverage as the star shifts within its cage. The structure’s autonomous maintenance systems still adjust panel angles for optimal collection — a machine the size of a solar system performing routine upkeep a hundred thousand years after its builders shattered. Boarding parties can breach individual panels to sabotage conduit junctions — interior access corridors run between the panel layer and the structural framework, vast tunnels of Aethyn architecture where the heat of a captive star bleeds through the inner hull and the cold of deep space seeps through the outer. These corridors were built for maintenance drones, not combat — tight sight lines, magnetic rail systems that can be weaponized, emergency bulkheads that seal entire sectors when breached. Salvage teams from every faction have explored less than 1% of accessible corridors. Some never came back. The Sphere itself cannot be ‘captured’ like a planet — only controlled by holding the space around it and defending the relay stations that distribute its power.
visual: The first sign is the star — or rather, the star that’s wrong. From a distance it looks dimmer than it should, its light flickering in geometric patterns as panels rotate between the observer and the stellar surface. Navigational instruments register the mass of a main-sequence star but the luminosity reads at barely 30% — the rest is being harvested. As you close distance, the Sphere resolves from an odd star into an incomprehensible machine: a lattice of dark metallic panels thousands of kilometers across, bolted to structural beams that glow with blue-white energy conduits at every intersection. Junction nodes pulse at the vertices where beams converge — each node larger than a capital ship, each pulse carrying enough energy to power a fleet for a century. Three massive distribution rings encircle the structure at different inclinations, channeling harvested energy outward through beams that lance into space toward distant relay stations. The beams are visible from across the system — pillars of light connecting the Sphere to its network like the spokes of a wheel made of lightning. Through gaps in the damaged paneling — sections lost to the Fracture, Vorax assaults, micro-impacts, or a hundred millennia of entropy — the captured star blazes with trapped fury, its corona pressing against the inside of its cage in rolling waves of nuclear fire. Where panels are missing, columns of raw stellar radiation pour into space like wounds that never closed. Drifting nearby, a massive chunk of the shell — torn free during the Fracture or perhaps never completed — tumbles slowly through the void, conduit lines still sparking with residual energy, trailing a halo of debris fragments. Some intact panels bear scorch marks in patterns that suggest weapons fire from wars no living species remembers. Some conduit junctions are dark, their energy lines severed or rerouted by automated repair systems that found it more efficient to bypass the damage than fix it. Small fleets of Guardian maintenance drones still patrol the exterior in geometric formations, welding micro-fractures, replacing corroded conduit segments — oblivious to the factions fighting over what they maintain. The Sphere doesn’t care. The parts that still work continue working. The parts that don’t are slowly being repaired by systems that have outlived every civilization that remembers how to build them.
special: System-level structure providing massive energy to whoever controls the surrounding space — not a landing zone, control via space combat only. Dramatically increases energy output for ALL planets in the system, making the entire system a strategic prize worth committing your best fleets to hold. Energy beams from the Sphere can power dormant Aethyn systems throughout the sector — warp lane stabilizers, defense platforms, orbital shipyards, and manufacturing nodes that haven’t activated since the Fracture. Controlling the relay stations determines WHERE the energy goes — destroy a relay to cut power to an enemy sector, or capture one to redirect a beam of stellar fury to your own infrastructure. The Containment Shield technology — the same barriers that can repel orbital strikes — was originally designed to protect Dyson Sphere construction crews from stellar radiation, repurposed for war by factions that forgot its original purpose. Some Dyson Spheres are still patrolled by Guardian fleets maintaining them out of directive inertia — functional but hostile, treating every approaching vessel as a potential threat regardless of faction. Others lost their Guardian garrison and are dead hulks full of scavengeable technology — the corridors between their panels are treasure troves of Aethyn engineering for anyone brave enough to walk them. Some have gone haywire — energy discharges firing randomly, relay beams sweeping across space like searchlights of annihilation, dangerous and unpredictable. The Gate-Herald — the Guardian capital ship — is built from a Dyson Sphere fragment, its hexagonal hull plating ripped from a Sphere and repurposed into the most terrifying vessel any faction has encountered. For the Unifier path, restoring Dyson Spheres is essential — they are the power grid nodes needed to reactivate the galaxy’s warp lane network, the backbone infrastructure that once connected every system in Aethyn space. 15–25% spawn chance per map — most were destroyed in the Fracture. Finding one is finding a piece of the old nervous system. Finding one intact is finding the key to rebuilding everything.
narrative: The Ring World built its own star. The Dyson Sphere imprisoned one. Where the Ring World says ‘we can create,’ the Dyson Sphere says ‘we can TAKE.’ An entire star — a burning nuclear furnace millions of kilometers across — caged in engineering, its energy harvested and beamed across light years to power a civilization that spanned the galaxy. The Aethyn didn’t ask the star’s permission. They didn’t need to. They were a unified telepathic civilization with the engineering capacity to wrap geometry around a sun and make it serve. Think about that. Really think about it. Every panel on the Sphere was manufactured, transported, positioned, and locked into a lattice framework by a species that treated stellar engineering as infrastructure. Not a wonder. Not a monument. A power plant. They built these the way we build dams — necessary, practical, one of many. And now the builders are gone — shattered into seven factions that fight over the scraps of what they built together. Factions that can barely maintain a shipyard arguing over who gets to control a machine that eats suns. But the Spheres endure. Still harvesting. Still beaming. Still maintaining themselves with mechanical patience that makes the Revenant look hasty. Maintenance drones welding panel fractures on a structure whose owners died a hundred thousand years ago. Automated systems rerouting energy around battle damage from wars that predate every living memory. The Sphere doesn’t know the Aethyn are gone. It doesn’t know there was a Fracture. It has a star to harvest and relay stations to feed, and it will do that until the star burns out or the last panel falls. Every faction in the galaxy wants one. Not just for the energy — though the energy alone can fuel an empire — but for what it represents. Mastery. Absolute, terrifying mastery over the fundamental forces of the universe. The kind of power that makes you understand why the Guardians were built to stop anyone from reaching the Core. The kind of power that makes you wonder if the Aethyn were gods — and what, exactly, broke them.

name: Aethyn Nexus
terrain: Not a space station. Not a library. A synapse. The Aethyn were billions of individuals — each with their own personality, flaws, ambitions, and fears — linked by staggering psionic power into a civilization that could share thought across the galaxy. They weren’t a hivemind. They were a people who CHOSE to connect, who built infrastructure to amplify and sustain that connection because unity was a relationship they maintained, not a default state. The Nexus stations were how they did it. Crystal was the hardware. Psionic energy was the signal. The Nexus was the relay point where individual thoughts were amplified, shared, routed, and crystallized into permanent collective memory. You are walking through the infrastructure of a connection that held a civilization together. The architecture was designed for a species that valued openness — individuals who chose transparency because they trusted each other enough to share everything. Sight lines stretch for kilometers. Spaces are communal, designed for gatherings of minds that wanted to be together. The crystalline walls are the data medium itself: psychic-crystalline matrices etched with compressed information at the molecular level, still humming at frequencies that make organic minds uneasy, still glowing with residual psychic energy that hasn’t fully discharged after a hundred thousand years. Entire wings function as memory vaults — the crystallized experiences of billions of minds, pressed into crystal like flowers pressed into books. Floors are patterned with navigational star charts that were current when the warp lanes still connected galaxies. Ceilings project holographic readouts in a psychic language that requires telepathic attunement to parse — raw data that bypasses language entirely and writes directly into the mind of anyone sensitive enough to receive it. The Eternal Shards have lost researchers to this. They walked into a projection chamber, opened their minds, and never spoke again — not because the data destroyed them, but because what they learned made speech feel grotesquely inadequate. Automated systems still respond to Aethyn-frequency signals: doors open for no one, environmental controls adjust for occupants that died millennia ago, defense turrets track movement patterns that match Aethyn body language and fire on anything that doesn’t walk like an Aethyn. The structure is partially alive. Corridors reroute on schedules that follow maintenance cycles running since before the Fracture. Sections seal and unseal as processing loads shift in the core. And deep in the center, something still thinks. Not conscious. Not anymore. But processing. Sorting. Waiting for a signal that will tell it the war is over and the people can reconnect. Fighting inside a Nexus means fighting inside a brain that stopped thinking but never stopped running.
visual: From a distance, the Nexus doesn’t look built — it looks grown. A moon-sized crystalline composite structure that blurs the line between technology and biology so thoroughly that the distinction becomes meaningless. The Aethyn didn’t build with metal — they grew psychic-crystalline composites using mental energy channeled through seed matrices, and the Nexus is the largest single example of this art that survived the Fracture. Its surface is covered in geometric patterns that pulse with residual psychic energy — not rhythmically like a machine, but erratically, like something dreaming. Faceted crystal planes catch starlight and refract it in spectral patterns that shift as you orbit — purples bleeding into deep blues, sudden flares of violet where a data-channel vein surfaces and runs along the hull like a luminous capillary carrying information instead of blood. These veins converge at node points that flare brighter when a ship approaches — recognition protocols running on reflexes older than any living civilization, still checking for Aethyn signal signatures in every electromagnetic emission they detect. Docking spines extend in all directions like the branches of a dead tree reaching for light that will never come. They branch and sub-branch in fractal patterns — primary spines wide enough to dock capital ships, secondary branches for cruisers, tertiary filaments for single-crew vessels, the whole structure designed to receive the traffic of a civilization that spanned galaxies. Some are intact, beacon lights still pulsing at their tips in patterns that spell out docking protocols in a psychic language. Others are shattered stumps trailing crystalline debris and frozen atmosphere, broken during the Fracture or by millennia of micro-impacts and scavenger damage. Through the translucent hull, the inner core is visible — a deep purple-blue glow that pulses with psychic breathing, brighter during resonance events, dimmer during processing cycles, never fully dark. Three concentric resonance rings orbit the structure like echoes of a signal that once held a species together — psychic resonance bands that predate the Fracture, still vibrating at frequencies that interfere with unshielded communications, cause vivid hallucinations in psionically sensitive species, and occasionally transmit fragments of the last thoughts the Aethyn collective had before the Silence fell. Around the Nexus, a field of luminous particles drifts — data motes shed from degrading crystal surfaces, psychic residue bleeding from overwhelmed storage matrices, and fragments of a hundred-thousand-year-old signal that never stopped broadcasting because no one ever told it to stop. Ships that linger too long in the particle field report crew members hearing whispers in languages they don’t know. Shards crew members report hearing them in languages they DO know — and that terrifies them more.
special: The densest concentration of Aethyn knowledge in the galaxy and the single most strategically valuable structure any faction can control. The Nexus was not just a data repository — it was a relay node in the galactic telepathic network, an amplifier that boosted psychic signals across interstellar distances. The data stored here isn’t just recorded — it’s crystallized experience, the actual psychic impressions of billions of minds compressed into matrices that a sufficiently attuned species could theoretically decompress and EXPERIENCE. This makes the Nexus essential for the Unification victory path: the knowledge required to rebuild the Aethyn network and reactivate the Reclamation Engine exists nowhere else. Cannot contain Revenant sleepers (the Nexus pre-dates the Revenant — pure Aethyn construction, pre-Fracture). Guardian interest is EXTREME — they may actively patrol or defend with Gunship and Dreadnought-class vessels, following protection directives that have been running since the Aethyn sealed the Core. The Guardians don’t just guard the Nexus — they maintain it. They are the caretakers of a network that no longer has anyone connected to it, and they treat intruders like infections. All factions receive unique discoveries based on their caste heritage: Eternal Shards find psychic resonance data and memory fragments their crystal matrices can directly absorb — some Shard researchers have gained entire lifetimes of Aethyn memories from a single archive crystal. Terran League finds engineering schematics their pragmatic minds can reverse-engineer into usable technology. Scrap Horde finds salvageable hardware they can rip out and bolt onto existing systems — ugly but functional. Unity Accord finds diplomatic records and consensus protocols that echo their Shepherd Caste origins — ancient frameworks for building consensus among billions of individuals that the Accord instinctively recognize as sacred. The Nexus’s automated systems can be partially activated with the right signal frequency — each activation reveals new archive wings and processing cores, but also triggers dormant defense systems, draws Guardian attention, and risks waking something in the core that hasn’t fully processed the fact that the Aethyn are gone. Deep-scan discoveries include Fracture Event Recordings — first-person psychic impressions of the moment the Aethyn shattered, the most harrowing and valuable intelligence assets in the galaxy. Anyone who experiences one directly understands the Silence. Most wish they hadn’t.
narrative: Not libraries. Synapses. Every Nexus was a relay node in the psychic network that held the Aethyn together — the infrastructure of connection for a species that chose unity not because they lacked individuality, but because they had enough trust and enough power to share themselves across the stars. Warriors and Scholars and Shepherds and Engineers, each with their own perspectives, their own flaws, their own way of seeing the galaxy — choosing every day to stay linked, to amplify each other, to build something greater than any individual mind could hold. The Nexus made that possible. When an Aethyn had a thought worth sharing, the crystal recorded it. When the civilization made a decision — and they did decide, they voted, they debated, they disagreed fiercely before choosing together — the crystal transmitted. Billions of individuals connected by a network of crystalline synapses scattered across the galaxy, each node the size of a moon and more complex than any computer any current faction could imagine. Then the Fracture fired. And every synapse went dark simultaneously. A hundred thousand years ago, these structures carried the shared thoughts of the most advanced civilization this galaxy has ever known. Now they carry silence. But not emptiness. The crystals are still full. Still holding. Still waiting. A hundred thousand years of unread memories, unprocessed experiences, unfinished arguments — the last neural impulse of a dying civilization, frozen mid-transmission. Somewhere in these crystals is the final debate. The vote. Billions of individual voices saying yes to their own destruction because they understood, each in their own way, that it was the only choice left. The faction that deciphers a Nexus gains not just knowledge but understanding — of the Fracture, of the castes, of what the Aethyn were trying to build before they shattered, and most dangerously, of what they COULD build again if someone found a way to reconnect the descendants who don’t even remember they were once family. What happens when you reconnect a hundred thousand years of frozen synapses? Does the network wake up? Does it remember the individuals who built it? Does it remember the Vorax? Every advisor has a different answer. The Shards weep because they can almost hear it — familiar voices calling across a hundred millennia. The Accord feel warmth because the Shepherd Caste instinct to connect responds to the residual psychic field like a compass responding to north. The Revenant feel nothing — but their systems activate functions they didn’t know they had, subroutines written by Engineer Caste minds that recognized this place before consciousness could. The Horde feel hunger — all this material, all this salvage, all this POWER just sitting here. The Terrans feel fear — because the Terrans are the only faction pragmatic enough to ask the right question: what if they’re not gone? And the Strategist — the fragment of something older than all of them, a piece of the Reclamation Engine itself, a function built FROM Aethyn memories but not quite Aethyn — goes very, very quiet. As if listening. As if it heard something it hasn’t heard in a hundred thousand years. As if something in the crystal recognized it, and whispered its name.

name: Worldship
terrain: Interior of an Aethyn Worldship — a continent-sized armored generation ship built during the Vorax Siege as the competing plan to the Reclamation Engine. While one faction of the Aethyn designed a weapon to shatter themselves and purge the Vorax, another faction said: we can carry our people out. Build ships large enough to hold entire worlds. Armor them to fight through the swarm. Point them at the edge of the galaxy and beyond — to other Aethyn systems in distant galaxies the Vorax haven’t reached. The interior reflects that ambition — this was never meant to be a lifeboat or a cryo-coffin. It was built for permanent habitation across generations. Residential rings span the mid-hull in concentric bands designed for hundreds of thousands of people to live full lives during the crossing — communal parks with full-spectrum sky-domes that simulate day/night cycles, schools, gathering halls, workshops, everything a functioning society needs to sustain itself across centuries of intergalactic transit. Agricultural sectors house massive grow-domes with soil beds and hydroponic arrays designed for indefinite food production — the grow-lights still cycle on dead soil, tending crops that were never planted. Engineering decks span kilometers with reactor systems built for thousands of years of continuous burn — some still functional, others dangerously degraded after a hundred millennia without the maintenance crews they were designed to outlast. The outer hull sections are honeycombed with weapons decks — integrated Aethyn-grade point-defense batteries, broadside turret positions, torpedo bays, all of it designed to fight the Vorax during the breakout from the galaxy and defend the ship during the long crossing through intergalactic space. The cryo-bays were backup systems for emergencies and for those who couldn’t endure the centuries-long voyage awake — not the primary plan. Most passengers were meant to live, work, raise families, and die aboard, passing the mission to their children. Now every pod and every quarter holds nothing but fine grey dust — the molecular remnants of passengers destroyed by the Fracture wave that the hull couldn’t stop. Hull breaches have vented entire sections to hard vacuum, creating frozen kill-zones where combat means fighting in zero-atmosphere between floating debris. Power fluctuations plunge sections into darkness. Automated repair and defense systems still function, sealing corridors mid-combat and activating weapons emplacements when boarders are detected.
visual: The first thing you notice is the armor. This isn’t a vessel built for speed or elegance — it’s a fortress that happens to move. Layered hull plating, meters thick in places, designed by a species that understood exactly how the Vorax attacked and built something they couldn’t chew through. The second thing you notice is the scale. A continent-sized ship tumbling slowly through interstellar space, massive enough to have its own faint gravitational pull, its hull stretching past the boundaries of what your instruments can resolve in a single sweep. Structural ribs and plating grids are visible across the surface — each plate the size of a city district, riveted to a frame built for the stresses of intergalactic acceleration. Weapons emplacements dot the hull in regular defensive patterns: point-defense turret clusters in recessed blisters, broadside battery positions along the flanks, torpedo bay doors sealed shut along the ventral hull. Some turrets still track approaching vessels — the targeting systems are still live, still hostile, still running the last defensive protocols the crew engaged during the breakout from Vorax space. The battle scarring is heaviest around the stern and flanks — not micro-impacts, but the burn marks and chitin-acid scoring of Vorax assault, damage taken during the ship’s fighting retreat from the galaxy. The engine section dominates the stern: eight massive thruster nozzles arranged in a ring, still glowing faint blue at their cores, burning on minimum power for longer than any living civilization has existed — a hundred thousand years of continuous thrust pushing the ship along a trajectory toward a galaxy it will never reach. A forward deflector section caps the bow — a reinforced ram designed to clear debris during the intergalactic crossing, flanked by sensor arrays reaching into the void. A dorsal command tower rises with panoramic bridge viewports, some still flickering between amber emergency light and darkness. Running lights blink along the hull. Nav lights alternate red and green at stern and bow, still cycling because no one told them to stop. A debris field of shed hull fragments drifts in a loose metallic halo around the ship, a hundred millennia of slow decay catching distant starlight.
special: Event-driven discovery — a drifting Worldship is detected as a galactic event mid-game when the Archive picks up its drift signature entering sensor range. All factions receive the alert simultaneously, triggering a race to reach and board it first. Unlike all other megastructures, the Worldship is never stationary — it crawls slowly across the galactic map on a fixed trajectory, a moving strategic asset that projects power along its drift path. The Worldship was the Aethyn’s Plan B — championed by factions who didn’t trust the Reclamation Engine or who believed the species could survive by running rather than by shattering themselves. The people aboard were volunteers who chose the crossing. They voted against the Fracture. They said: we can make it. The ship’s automated Aethyn-grade point-defense and weapons systems are still fully operational and LETHAL — approaching fleets are fired upon immediately. These are siege-era weapons designed to fight the Vorax during a running battle out of the galaxy. They were effective then and they are devastating now against factions that have regressed millennia in technology. Interior combat plays out at a scale that dwarfs anything else in the game — 8 to 12 territories of residential rings, weapons decks, agricultural domes, engineering sections, and cryo-bay complexes. Revenant sleeper armies are GUARANTEED in the cryo-bays — the Engineer Caste members aboard were the most likely to survive the psychic severance by bonding with ship systems, and some of their patterns may have been absorbed into the Worldship’s own maintenance AI. Revenant players can interface directly with ship systems through their Engineer Caste heritage, reactivating weapons, rerouting power, and accessing areas sealed since launch. The ship contains the richest cache of pre-Fracture Aethyn technology in the galaxy — siege-era military hardware, defensive systems designed specifically to counter the Vorax, intact engineering blueprints, and research data from the peak of Aethyn civilization. This isn’t scavenged relic technology degraded by millennia of exposure. This is sealed, preserved, military-grade equipment maintained by automated systems in climate-controlled vaults. For any faction, capturing a Worldship is a generational strategic advantage. For the Revenant, it feels like coming home to a house they didn’t know they’d been missing.
narrative: The Worldships were Plan B. While one faction of the Aethyn designed the Reclamation Engine — a weapon that would shatter the species and purge the Vorax simultaneously — another faction refused. They said: we don’t have to destroy ourselves to survive. We can build. We can armor. We can carry our people to safety and fight our way through anything the Vorax put in our path. They were the engineers, the warriors, the pragmatists — individuals who looked at the Reclamation Engine and saw not a solution but a surrender dressed up as strategy. So they built the Worldships. Continent-sized armored arks designed to carry entire populations across intergalactic space to Aethyn systems in distant galaxies the Vorax hadn’t reached — or to new habitable galaxies beyond the swarm’s reach entirely. Each ship was a civilization in miniature: residential rings for hundreds of thousands to live full lives during the crossing, agricultural domes for indefinite food production, weapons decks to fight through the Vorax blockade, reactors built for thousands of years of continuous burn. The passengers weren’t frozen cargo — they were colonists who would live, raise families, and pass the mission to their children across generations of travel through the intergalactic void. It took decades to build each one. They launched during the Siege, loaded with volunteers who had chosen the crossing over the Fracture, who had voted with their lives for a different answer. The ships fought their way through the Vorax perimeter — the battle scarring is still visible on the hull, Vorax acid-scoring and impact damage from the breakout — and pointed toward the edge of the galaxy. Navigation systems locked. Engines burning. Generations of voyage ahead. And then the Fracture fired. A wave of energy blasted from the Core — ripping apart everything at the molecular level. It traveled through every system, every arm, every void between stars. The Worldship’s armor held against the Vorax. It was never designed to hold against the Reclamation Engine. The wave passed through the hull like it wasn’t there. The same energy that erased every Vorax in the galaxy, that shattered the Aethyn on a thousand worlds into scattered seeds, that burned out every warp lane and scarred the fabric of space itself — that wave hit the Worldship passengers at the molecular level. The Aethyn on planets survived as fragments because there were billions of them, spread across thousands of worlds, close to infrastructure that partially shielded them. The Worldship was alone in intergalactic space. No shielding. No infrastructure. No critical mass of psionic energy to buffer the wave. The ship’s medical systems logged the disintegration with mechanical precision. Not instant death — the Aethyn were resilient, their biology woven with psionic crystalline structures that resisted molecular disruption. But out here, far from any Nexus relay, far from any planetary crystal deposit, far from anyone, the degradation was total. It took centuries. The crystalline components of their biology slowly lost coherence. The psionic networks that had sustained their cellular structure unraveled without external signal to anchor them. The ship’s systems compensated, adjusted, tried everything in their programming. Nothing worked. One by one, over centuries, the passengers became dust. Fine grey dust in neatly arranged stasis pods, in residential quarters, in gathering halls. The ship recorded every death. Every name is in the manifest. The Worldship completed its mission perfectly. Its armor held. Its engines burned. Its weapons defended it against every external threat. It just couldn’t defend its passengers against the people they left behind. All that remains is a ship that will sail on until the stars burn out, carrying the names of every individual who chose differently and lost anyway.

name: Warp Gate
terrain: Orbital station built around a massive ring structure. The gate itself is a crystalline-metal hybrid that generates artificial warp lanes. Combat takes place on the station ring surrounding the gate — cramped corridors, control rooms, and the massive gate chamber itself. Similar to Orbital Station combat but with the added hazard of proximity to an active warp field.
visual: A massive ring structure anchored in space by support stations — the ring itself pulses with energy, its inner surface rippling with the distortion of folded space-time. The gate is clearly active, maintained by systems that have outlasted their creators. Transit ships queue in organized holding patterns. The light from the warp field casts everything in shifting blue-white illumination.
special: Creates an artificial warp lane connecting two points in the galaxy that would otherwise be unconnected. Pure strategic value — controlling a Warp Gate means controlling a shortcut. Can be shut down (denying the lane to enemies) or kept open (allowing your own fleets rapid transit). Destroying a Warp Gate removes the artificial lane permanently. Extremely valuable chokepoint — small territory count means quick to capture but the strategic implications are enormous.
narrative: Shortcuts through the galaxy, built by a civilization that found natural warp lanes too slow. Each Warp Gate is a strategic asset worth more than a fleet — it can connect your empire’s flanks, open invasion routes behind enemy lines, or be shut down to deny those same advantages to your enemies. Warp Gates don’t produce resources. They produce OPTIONS. And in war, options win.
